issue_title: "ValueError: No outputs can be identified" when converting Keras Tensorflow model with coremltools
issue_body:
The latest changes ( [5b9ca1a](https://github.com/apple/coremltools/commit/5b9ca1a2a58ebe169f91bc0ea3f30e1cc4c62bc0) ), to [_topology2.py](https://github.com/apple/coremltools/blob/master/coremltools/converters/keras/_topology2.py), has fixed the index out out range issue but it seems that there is still a problem with detecting the input and output layers/names. 

The conversion script:
```
import sys
sys.path.insert(0, r'/Users/corneel/GitHub/KerasTests2')
import coremltools
from keras.models import load_model
model = load_model('final_model.h5')
print("Loaded")

coreml_model = coremltools.converters.keras.convert(model,
    input_names="input_1",
    image_input_names="input_1",
    output_names="sequential_1",
    add_custom_layers=True)

coreml_model.save('final3.mlmodel')
```
Traceback (most recent call last):
```
File "Convert.py", line 15, in <module> add_custom_layers=True)
File "/Users/corneel/GitHub/KerasTests2/coremltools/converters/keras/_keras_converter.py" line 745, in convert custom_conversion_functions=custom_conversion_functions)

File "/Users/corneel/GitHub/KerasTests2/coremltools/converters/keras/_topology2.py", line 185, in make_output_layers raise ValueError("No outputs can be identified")
ValueError: No outputs can be identified
```
The failure is as a result of this piece of code in [_topology2.py](https://github.com/apple/coremltools/blob/master/coremltools/converters/keras/_topology2.py)
![](https://user-images.githubusercontent.com/356575/34276364-bcdbcbce-e666-11e7-9a6a-8b14a335781d.png)

I was follow the guidance in the article: "https://blog.keras.io/building-powerful-image-classification-models-using-very-little-data.html" by @fchollet (author of Keras). The model is a [VGG-like convnet ](https://faroit.github.io/keras-docs/2.0.6/getting-started/sequential-model-guide/), seeded with a pretrained network (imagenet). As far as I can see my network behaves perfectly, the extra images I loaded is detected (predicted) and any report I run on this network, completes without any problem. I was also careful to use the versions of software you prescribed ([pip_freeze.txt](https://github.com/apple/coremltools/files/1580675/pip_freeze.txt) (coremltools does not show there because I use a compiled version)). 

I attached a [visual display](https://user-images.githubusercontent.com/356575/34275555-778c5d0c-e663-11e7-8f34-7373395dcfcd.png) of the network, as well as the _json_ and _yaml_ reports. [GitHub_Issue_No-outputs-can-be-identified.zip](https://github.com/apple/coremltools/files/1580777/GitHub_Issue_No-outputs-can-be-identified.zip)

Here is the Keras summary of the model 
[Keras_model_summary.txt](https://github.com/apple/coremltools/files/1580690/Keras_model_summary.txt)

This model was create using the following script as provided by @fchollet:
```
from keras import applications
from keras.preprocessing.image import ImageDataGenerator
from keras import optimizers
from keras.models import Sequential
from keras.models import Model
from keras.layers import Dropout, Flatten, Dense

# path to the model weights files.
weights_path = '../keras/examples/vgg16_weights.h5'
top_model_weights_path = 'bottleneck_fc_model.h5'
# dimensions of our images.
img_width, img_height = 150, 150

train_data_dir = 'data/train'
validation_data_dir = 'data/validation'
nb_train_samples = 2000
nb_validation_samples = 800
epochs = 50
batch_size = 16

# build the VGG16 network
base_model = applications.VGG16(weights='imagenet', include_top=False, input_shape=(150,150,3))
print('Model loaded.')

# build a classifier model to put on top of the convolutional model
top_model = Sequential()
top_model.add(Flatten(input_shape=base_model.output_shape[1:]))
top_model.add(Dense(256, activation='relu'))
top_model.add(Dropout(0.5))
top_model.add(Dense(1, activation='sigmoid'))

# note that it is necessary to start with a fully-trained
# classifier, including the top classifier,
# in order to successfully do fine-tuning
top_model.load_weights(top_model_weights_path)

# add the model on top of the convolutional base
# model.add(top_model)
model = Model(inputs=base_model.input, outputs=top_model(base_model.output))

# set the first 25 layers (up to the last conv block)
# to non-trainable (weights will not be updated)
for layer in model.layers[:15]:
    layer.trainable = False

# compile the model with a SGD/momentum optimizer
# and a very slow learning rate.
model.compile(loss='binary_crossentropy',
              optimizer=optimizers.SGD(lr=1e-4, momentum=0.9),
              metrics=['accuracy'])

# prepare data augmentation configuration
train_datagen = ImageDataGenerator(
    rescale=1. / 255,
    shear_range=0.2,
    zoom_range=0.2,
    horizontal_flip=True)

test_datagen = ImageDataGenerator(rescale=1. / 255)

train_generator = train_datagen.flow_from_directory(
    train_data_dir,
    target_size=(img_height, img_width),
    batch_size=batch_size,
    class_mode='binary')

validation_generator = test_datagen.flow_from_directory(
    validation_data_dir,
    target_size=(img_height, img_width),
    batch_size=batch_size,
    class_mode='binary')

model.summary()

# fine-tune the model
model.fit_generator(
    train_generator,
    steps_per_epoch=nb_train_samples // batch_size,
    epochs=epochs,
    validation_data=validation_generator,
    validation_steps=nb_validation_samples // batch_size,
    verbose=2)

model.predict_function

model.save
```
In the script the output is distinctly identified:

> model = Model(inputs=base_model.input, outputs=top_model(base_model.output))

Why would the convertor not recognize the output? (Sequential) layer?

issue_title: Validation happens after hydration
issue_body:
Validation happens after hydration, [as it turnes out](https://github.com/django-tastypie/django-tastypie/blob/master/tastypie/resources.py#L2336). I didn't expect that, and this seems misleading. Also [docs](http://django-tastypie.readthedocs.io/en/latest/validation.html) don't mention this behavior.

I've expected user submitted data to be validated first and then hydrated. In my case I check timestamp in user request, and I should get HTTP400 response right as soon as I see that timestamp is invalid. If I do hydration first, I have to make that check in `hydrate()` method, which seems to be a wrong place to do so.

To make my API behave as I expect, I had to add these lines to my `hydrate()` method before any real hydration:
```python
    self.is_valid(bundle)
    if bundle.errors:
        raise ImmediateHttpResponse(response=self.error_response(bundle.request, bundle.errors))
```

And after that I got HTTP400 for bad request as I shoud have. But validation actually ran twice.

Is there any hidden meaning in running validation just before saving object, that I don't get?

P.S. Sorry for my bad english, not my native language =/

issue_title: Allow Meta.fields to be empty list to specify no field introspection
issue_body:
> fields
Controls what introspected fields the Resource should include. A whitelist of fields. Default is []

Please mention that if Meta.fields == [] it means that ALL Django model fields are introspected. To simulate no introspection you can do `Meta.fields = ['']`
http://stackoverflow.com/questions/14597332/tastypie-custom-fields

issue_title: use development branches for larger features and changes
issue_body:
Is the pvlib community interested in using development branches for larger features and changes? The idea is to get relatively large proposals in the hands of more people before they're merged. For example, #124 is a large feature addition and #151 breaks the existing API, so they require a 0.x version change to merge instead of only a 0.x.y change. More importantly, I am reluctant to merge large changes without more significant user feedback. 

But critical user feedback is harder to get if it requires navigating the git maze. **If** you're comfortable using git then it's very easy (and powerful) to checkout another user's PR to try for yourself, but if you're newish to git and using GitHub Desktop or similar then it's somewhere between a mystery and impossible to do that. I think (hope?) that newish users of git and GitHub Desktop can switch branches of a single remote repository without too much trouble.

How would this work in practice? All features would still start out as PRs against master. Depending on the scope, we may decide to add it as a new branch once the code is useable. One of the maintainers would have to complete that step. The original PR can then be closed, and a new PR can be opened from the development branch to the master branch. The original developer can still submit his/her own PRs against the development branch. The maintainers can quickly merge these PRs with abandon since they don't impact master. Once everything is good, the development branch can be merged into master and deleted. 

Either way, I'm planning to push a development branch for the forecasting/expanded-io-module PR tomorrow because I think it will make accessing the code/documentation for my PVSC paper/poster slightly less confusing.

Thoughts?

@jforbess and @cwhanse might have some perspective on this.

issue_title: Adding a rule reduces the cover of a predicate
issue_body:
In this program, the query returns a bunch of results for the query when the final rule is commented out, but when the rule is there, the query returns a single result.  


    (define-sort Bv8 () (_ BitVec 8))
    (define-sort Bv32 () (_ BitVec 32))
    (declare-rel a (Bv8 Bv8 Bv32))

    (rule (a #x00 #x00 #x0A000001))
    (rule (a #x01 #x00 #x0A000002))
    (rule (a #x02 #x01 #x0A000101))
    (rule (a #x03 #x01 #x0A000102))
    (rule (a #x04 #x01 #x0A010001))
    (rule (a #x05 #x01 #x0A010002))
    (rule (a #x06 #x01 #x0A010101))
    (rule (a #x07 #x01 #x0A010102))
    (rule (a #x08 #x02 #x0A020001))

    (declare-rel b (Bv8  Bv8 Bv8 Bv8 Bool))
    (declare-rel d (Bv8))
    (declare-rel e (Bv8))

    (rule (forall ((A Bv8) (B Bv8) (C Bv8) (D Bool))
                  (=> (and
                       (b A B C #x00 D)
                       (d C))
                      (e A))))

    (query (exists ((X Bv8) (Y Bv8) (Z Bv32)) (a X Y Z)) :print-answer true)
        

Without last rule:

    $ z3 z3/bug-example/bug.smt2
    sat
    (or (and (= X!1 #x00) (= X!2 #x00) (= X!3 #x0a000001))
        (and (= X!1 #x01) (= X!2 #x00) (= X!3 #x0a000002))
        (and (= X!1 #x02) (= X!2 #x01) (= X!3 #x0a000101))
        (and (= X!1 #x03) (= X!2 #x01) (= X!3 #x0a000102))
        (and (= X!1 #x04) (= X!2 #x01) (= X!3 #x0a010001))
        (and (= X!1 #x05) (= X!2 #x01) (= X!3 #x0a010002))
        (and (= X!1 #x06) (= X!2 #x01) (= X!3 #x0a010101))
        (and (= X!1 #x07) (= X!2 #x01) (= X!3 #x0a010102))
        (and (= X!1 #x08) (= X!2 #x02) (= X!3 #x0a020001)))

With the rule:

issue_title: Reaching definition in thumb mode
issue_body:
Currently reaching definition in ARM thumb node is not very reliable, because of the implementation of vex in thumb mode. 

For example, given the following instruction:
`00414CC6 49 46       MOV             R1, R9`

The vex with the optimization level 0 (as reaching definition has it):
`block = p.factory.block(0x00414CC6, opt_level=0, thumb=True)`
`block.vex.pp()`
Output:
```
   00 | ------ IMark(0x414cc6, 2, 1) ------
   01 | t0 = 0x00000000
   02 | PUT(itstate) = t0
   03 | t1 = 0x00000001
   04 | t2 = GET:I32(v6)
   05 | t7 = GET:I32(r1)
   06 | t8 = CmpNE32(t1,0x00000000)
   07 | t6 = ITE(t8,t2,t7)
   08 | PUT(r1) = t6
   09 | PUT(ip) = 0x00414cc9
```
We can see that R1, that should be dependent only on R9 (v6) [line 04 in vex], also dependent on it's previous definition [line 05 in vex], when it clearly seen that it will never use it as a definition.

issue_title: the heartbeat check should not be ON by default
issue_body:
We currently impose our heartbeat checks on all apps that use DroneKit, we should aim to give developers the freedom to deal with the heartbeat issues on their own.

I propose the following:
- heartbeat check is disabled by default
- expose heartbeat timeout callbacks
- cleanup the current heartbeat checks, it's not 100% clear and can cause confusion when reading

Since this is a breaking change, we should aim to have this by v3.0.0

issue_title: 关于StockCommission中的get_commission的疑问
issue_body:
在rqalpha/mod/rqalpha_mod_sys_simulation/decider/commission.py中的StockCommission类的get_commission方法中用到了self.commission_map,  其计算手续费的代码如下:

```
        commission = self.commission_map[order_id]
        cost_money = trade.last_price * trade.last_quantity * self.rate * self.multiplier
        if cost_money > commission:
            if commission == self.min_commission:
                self.commission_map[order_id] = 0
                return cost_money
            else:
                self.commission_map[order_id] = 0
                return cost_money - commission
        else:
            if commission == self.min_commission:
                self.commission_map[order_id] -= cost_money
                return commission
            else:
                self.commission_map[order_id] -= cost_money
                return 0
```

上述代码中使用了一个self.commission_map, 个人感觉没有意义, 因为一个Order只生成一个Trade, 所以计算手续费可以直接使用如下代码:

```
        cost_money = trade.last_price * trade.last_quantity * self.rate * self.multiplier
        if cost_money > self.min_commission:
            return cost_money
        else:
            return self.min_commission
```

如果一个Order是分多个交易成交的, 那么应该通过该Orde已成交的总数量来计算该Order的手续费, 
而不是分别计算每次Trade的手续费, 
一个Order多次交易的手续费应该以Order为单位、而不是以Trade为单位来计算.

issue_title: CM exit function does not follow convention to return True or False
issue_body:
The Python description for the `__exit__()` function of context managers (see [here](https://docs.python.org/2/library/stdtypes.html?highlight=context%20manager#contextmanager.__exit__)) states that the `__exit__()` function should return `True` to indicate that exceptions raised during the context manager body should be suppressd, or `False` to indicate that such exceptions should be passed through.

the current implementation of the `__exit__()` function in the `Spinner` class returns nothing (i.e. `None`) and thus does not follow the documented convention. This does not currently hurt, because apparently the calling Python runtime code (at least in CPython 2.7) treats `None` in the same way as it would treat `False`, but nevertheless the Spinner code does not follow the documented convention and thus might show a different behaviour in a different Python implementation.

Solution: Return `False` in the `__exit__()` method. This assumes that we have consensus that exceptions in the context manager body should be passed through and should not be suppressed.

Rob

issue_title: Performance of plural endpoint without a million of objects
issue_body:
Reaching a plural endpoint with a million record should be super fast when using pagination (`?_limit=10`) or filtering.  Apparently, it's not the case.

I suspect https://github.com/Kinto/kinto/pull/1267 to be responsible for the regression.

> See https://github.com/mozilla-services/buildhub/issues/350 (840K records)

For anyone interested in tackling this, here is a quick way to fill up a collection with fake records:

```
$ psql -h localhost -U postgres -w


DO
$$
BEGIN
    FOR i IN 1..100000 LOOP
       INSERT INTO records (id, parent_id, collection_id) VALUES ('id-' || i, '/buckets/a/collections/b', 'record');
    END LOOP;
END;
$$;
```

issue_body:

I have a process flow where I want to programmatically call `pytraj.iterload()` on a list of trajectory files and, if they have a non-zero number of frames, do something with them. `Error: Could not set up trajectory '<traj>'`
`Error: NetCDF file has no frames.`

I can catch this error by simply checking if the iterload object exists after I attempt to create it, but I haven't found a way to suppress the internal error, which is cluttering my output stream. Thank you!

5. merge 2.bad and 1.

6. concat 5 and 2.good

issue_title: S3 delete_many swallows errors
issue_body: boto's [`delete_keys`](http://boto.cloudhackers.com/en/latest/ref/s3.html#boto.s3.bucket.Bucket.delete_keys) function doesn't error out, instead it returns a list of outcomes. flask annex should inspect the list of outcomes and throw if an error occurred. It should only swallow if the error is a "file not found"

issue_title: CREATE TABLE without IF NOT EXISTS fails
issue_body:
%%sql  
     CREATE TABLE bar (
       baz int
     )

This fails with this error:

     An error occurred while calling o56.sql.
: org.apache.spark.sql.execution.QueryExecutionException: FAILED: Execution Error, return code 1 from org.apache.hadoop.hive.ql.exec.DDLTask. AlreadyExistsException(message:Table bar already exists)

The SQL query actually gets executed twice (once to figure out the dataframe has no data, and another time to get the column names).  But it fails to execute the query the second time because the table was created the first time.

You can work around this issue by doing

    %%sql
    CREATE TABLE IF NOT EXISTS bar (...

... but it would be ideal if we could avoid this altogether.

I do not know if it's possible to do queries like `INSERT` in Hive, but this would also affect that by running the `INSERT` query twice.

issue_title: Unix builds not using distro provided dependencies
issue_body:
See: [commit](https://github.com/IntelRealSense/librealsense/commit/d77cfb41354b72ee30aa2fe4148355f3462dee83#diff-47fb227e42855c6a2e12cdd32e7f126e)

In the above commit Unix builds have moved to using third party libraries (such as libusb) provided in the 'third-party' directory of the repository. This may be required for Android building but when compiling a shared library for Unix the preferred method is to compile against distro provided libraries using pkgconfig to find these (as was the case prior to the Android changes).

Forcing people to compile librealsense like this goes completely against Unix norms and also breaks the build for Yocto!!!

### Issue Description
I checked some similar issues (https://github.com/IntelRealSense/librealsense/issues/901, https://communities.intel.com/thread/122871, https://github.com/IntelRealSense/librealsense/issues/927) but wasn't able to resolve mine, so I'm posting a new one.

I've just received three D435 cameras and I've been testing them out with RealSense Viewer. I'm using USB 3.0 (might be 3.1, not sure. They show up as USB 3.1 on the Viewer) to connect all three cameras, and I'm using the 1m passive USB 3.0 cables that came with them.

The stereo modules have been pretty unreliable, and I'm wondering how I can make them more reliable.

What's happening is that all the modules were working fine one minute, but when I closed and started up RealSense Viewer again, all the depth streams were blank, and it said "No Frames Received!" The IR and RGB streams were fine.
 
**Fig. 1 Three cameras working fine (but see "incomplete frame detected"):**

issue_title: More efficient way of aligning images
issue_body:
The current method of aligning images is highly CPU-intensive, involving repeated function calls to rs2_deproject_pixel_to_point, rs2_transform_point_to_point, and rs2_project_point_to_pixel on every point in the image. At 30fps this can consume as much as 50% of a core for just depth-RGB alone, let alone IR images.

Can we have a hardware-accelerated function that can align an entire image in one go instead of millions of repeated function calls per second? Better yet, can this be done in firmware?

issue_title: Documentation unclear.
issue_body:
The other day, my group (Sanic) had a discussion about graphene and we agree that the documentation is a little unpleasant (pardon my euphemism. Actual: **Terrible**). It's hard to understand and everything is briefly covered. I think there is a need to cover more ground in the documentation or maybe provide video examples (graphene has no tutorials on YouTube other than talks). I think many would agree based on the issue submitted by the community asking documentation-ish questions.

For an example, under the mutation section, the only defination for mutation is "**A Mutation is a special ObjectType that also defines an Input"**. This doesnt really tell much about mutation. I know that it is expected for your user to read the docs from graphql's official website, but it is also expected to have all the information present in one documentation as well without jumping from one docs to another.

issue_title: GraphQL and Sanic  integration
issue_body:
Hi @syrusakbary!

You might've heard about [Sanic](https://github.com/channelcat/sanic) web framework that utilizes new eventloop functionality on top of high perfomant uvloop and mimics Flask's API. It seems to be really interesting and promising project so far, even though it's in the pre-alfa status.

Thus, I've implemented [Sanic-GraphQL](https://github.com/grazor/sanic-graphql) bindings basing on the [Flask-GraphQL](https://github.com/graphql-python/flask-graphql) project. I'm certain that it's already stable enough to use and I'm going to keep it up to date in future. However, I believe that it would be better to transfer this project to the graphql-python organization in order to make it easier to discover.

I've tried to make my fork as legally correct as possible, although I don't really care much about my copyrights here and I can transfer this project to you and allow you publish it under your own name if you want.

issue_title: SerializerMutation creates excess types for firlds with same serializer class
issue_body:
I use ``SerializerMutation`` to create/update objects:

```python
from graphene_django.rest_framework.mutation import SerializerMutation

class ShipmentMutation(SerializerMutation):
    class Meta:
        serializer_class = ShipmentSerializer
```

Here is ``ShipmentSerializer``:

```python
from rest_framework import serializers

from logistics.models import City
from logistics.serializers import CitySerializer

from .models import Shipment


class ShipmentSerializer(serializers.ModelSerializer):
    owner = serializers.PrimaryKeyRelatedField(read_only=True)
    origin = CitySerializer(read_only=True)
    destination = CitySerializer(read_only=True)

    class Meta:
        model = Shipment
        exclude = ('created_at', 'updated_at', )
```

...and ``CitySerializer``:

```python
class CitySerializer(serializers.ModelSerializer):
    class Meta:
        model = City
        fields = ('id', 'name', )
        read_only_fields = ('id', 'name', )
```

issue_title: Incorrectly parse versions of some apps
issue_body:
I saw that for `django-celery-beat` and `django-celery-results` it shows extra dots at the end of the version:
```
django_celery_beat	Django_Celery_Beat	1.0.1..
django_celery_results	Django_Celery_Results	1.0.1..
```

This is because both apps use custom `namedtuple`:
```
version_info_t = namedtuple('version_info_t', (
    'major', 'minor', 'micro', 'releaselevel', 'serial',
))
```

When `releaselevel` and `serial` are not set they are empty strings. This will lead to joining dots in the string representation.

I'm preparing a fix and will create a PR.

issue_title: Consider switching from bleach to html-sanitizer
issue_body:
`bleach`'s dependency on `html5lib` has exhibited some pip-related issues:
https://github.com/tensorflow/tensorboard/issues/588

And it's also hard to upgrade:
https://github.com/tensorflow/tensorboard/issues/427

`html-sanitizer` seems to depend on `lxml` and `beautifulsoup4`. Using `html-sanitizer` might do away with those issues. Functionally, `html-sanitizer` also lets us filter for tags and attributes.
https://pypi.python.org/pypi/html-sanitizer/

Apple LLVM version 9.1.0 (clang-902.0.39.1)
Target: x86_64-apple-darwin17.5.0
Thread model: posix
InstalledDir: /Applications/Xcode.app/Contents/Developer/Toolchains/XcodeDefault.xctoolchain/usr/bin
```

```
❯ g++ -v
Configured with: --prefix=/Applications/Xcode.app/Contents/Developer/usr --with-gxx-include-dir=/usr/include/c++/4.2.1
Apple LLVM version 9.1.0 (clang-902.0.39.1)
Target: x86_64-apple-darwin17.5.0
Thread model: posix
InstalledDir: /Applications/Xcode.app/Contents/Developer/Toolchains/XcodeDefault.xctoolchain/usr/bin
```

## Steps to produce the error

```
❯ git rev-parse --short HEAD
52644515
❯ make -f examples/Makefile-posix
<snip>
❯ cd build/x86_64-apple-darwin
build/x86_64-apple-darwin❯ make check
<snip>
make[4]: `test-toolchain' is up to date.
  CXX      test_diag.o
  CXXLD    test-diag
Undefined symbols for architecture x86_64:
  "_gPlatformPseudoResetWasRequested", referenced from:
      _PlatformInit in libopenthread-posix.a(libopenthread_posix_a-platform.o)
      _PlatformPseudoResetWasRequested in libopenthread-posix.a(libopenthread_posix_a-platform.o)
ld: symbol(s) not found for architecture x86_64
clang: error: linker command failed with exit code 1 (use -v to see invocation)
make[4]: *** [test-diag] Error 1
<snip>
```

## Symbol living in the common section

Inspection of the archive shows that the missing symbol _is_ present in the misc.o object file in the _common_ section. It is also undefined in the platform.o object file. This corresponds with the definition of the variable in misc.c file and the extern declaration in the platform.c file.

```
❯ nm examples/platforms/posix/libopenthread-posix.a | grep -B14 _gPlatformPseudoResetWasRequested
<snip>
../../examples/platforms/posix/libopenthread-posix.a(libopenthread_posix_a-misc.o):
                 U _PlatformDeinit
                 U _alarm
                 U _execvp
                 U _exit
                 U _gArguments
                 U _gArgumentsCount
0000000000001120 b _gPlatMcuPowerState
0000000000000001 C _gPlatformPseudoResetWasRequested
--
../../examples/platforms/posix/libopenthread-posix.a(libopenthread_posix_a-platform.o):
00000000000002c4 D _NODE_ID
0000000000000140 T _PlatformDeinit
0000000000000000 T _PlatformInit
0000000000000150 T _PlatformProcessDrivers
0000000000000130 T _PlatformPseudoResetWasRequested
00000000000002c8 D _WELLKNOWN_NODE_ID
                 U ___error
                 U ___stderrp
                 U _basename
                 U _exit
                 U _fprintf
00000000000011e0 S _gArguments
00000000000011d8 S _gArgumentsCount
                 U _gPlatformPseudoResetWasRequested
```

(`nm` documentation quotes from https://sourceware.org/binutils/docs/binutils/nm.html)

> The symbol is common. Common symbols are uninitialized data. When linking, multiple common symbols may appear with the same name. If the symbol is defined anywhere, the common symbols are treated as undefined references. For more details on common symbols, see the discussion of –warn-common in Linker options in The GNU linker.

This prompted me to *add an initialization* to the definition in `misc.c`.

```
 48 bool                       gPlatformPseudoResetWasRequested = false;
```

Which fixes the issue. Upon further inspection we can also see that the variable no longer lives in the _common_ section, but in a section marked 'S':
> The symbol is in an uninitialized data section for small objects.

```
❯ nm examples/platforms/posix/libopenthread-posix.a | grep -B14 _gPlatformPseudoResetWasRequested
<snip>
../../examples/platforms/posix/libopenthread-posix.a(libopenthread_posix_a-misc.o):
                 U _PlatformDeinit
                 U _alarm
                 U _execvp
                 U _exit
                 U _gArguments
                 U _gArgumentsCount
0000000000001120 b _gPlatMcuPowerState
000000000000111e S _gPlatformPseudoResetWasRequested
--
../../examples/platforms/posix/libopenthread-posix.a(libopenthread_posix_a-platform.o):
00000000000002c4 D _NODE_ID
0000000000000140 T _PlatformDeinit
0000000000000000 T _PlatformInit
0000000000000150 T _PlatformProcessDrivers
0000000000000130 T _PlatformPseudoResetWasRequested
00000000000002c8 D _WELLKNOWN_NODE_ID
                 U ___error
                 U ___stderrp
                 U _basename
                 U _exit
                 U _fprintf
00000000000011e0 S _gArguments
00000000000011d8 S _gArgumentsCount
                 U _gPlatformPseudoResetWasRequested
```

## But the examples link properly!?

The problem only occurs when compiling this specific unit test, but not with the example applications. This seemed quite odd to me because the linker error above only relates to objects within a single archive, so I investigated further.

By running `make V=1` and `make V=1 check` I isolated the specific libtool commands used to link a cli app and the failing unit test respectively. I added diagnostic flags to the linker and ran the commands again.

- [vanilla_notfound.log](https://github.com/openthread/openthread/files/2029111/vanilla_notfound.log) shows that the linker fails to load the misc.o object file, which of course results in the symbol not being found.

- [vanilla_all_load.log](https://github.com/openthread/openthread/files/2029164/vanilla_all_load.log) shows that forcing the linker to load all objects results in a successful link.

```
<snip>
../../examples/platforms/posix/libopenthread-posix.a(libopenthread_posix_a-misc.o)
<snip>
```

- [initialized_found.log](https://github.com/openthread/openthread/files/2029167/initialized_found.log) shows that with the modification described above (initialize the global variable) directly causes the linker to load the misc.o object file.

```
<snip>
../../examples/platforms/posix/libopenthread-posix.a(libopenthread_posix_a-misc.o)
_gPlatformPseudoResetWasRequested forced load of ../../examples/platforms/posix/libopenthread-posix.a(libopenthread_posix_a-misc.o)
<snip>
```

- [cli_link.log](https://github.com/openthread/openthread/files/2029188/cli_link.log) shows that in the case of the cli application, another piece of code causes the misc.o object file to be loaded.

```
<snip>
../../../examples/platforms/posix/libopenthread-posix.a(libopenthread_posix_a-misc.o)
_otPlatReset forced load of ../../../examples/platforms/posix/libopenthread-posix.a(libopenthread_posix_a-misc.o)
<snip>
```

- [platreset_found.log](https://github.com/openthread/openthread/files/2029215/platreset_found.log) shows that hacking this behaviour into `test_diag.cpp` also fixes the linker issue. With the following bit added to the end of the file (and _without_ initializing the global variable):

```
void unused() {
    otPlatReset(NULL);
}
```

```
<snip>
../../examples/platforms/posix/libopenthread-posix.a(libopenthread_posix_a-misc.o)
_otPlatReset forced load of ../../examples/platforms/posix/libopenthread-posix.a(libopenthread_posix_a-misc.o)
<snip>
```

## Summary

It seems that there are multiple ways in which this problem can be solved. I am not sure which is the best way to go, but I will share my personal opinions below:

I think that tricking the linker to include some object files (as accidentally happens with the application linking process) is not a solution to the core problem.

Reading the discussion for the `--warn-common` linker option on https://sourceware.org/binutils/docs/ld/Options.html#Options leads me to believe that this is not a feature that should be relied upon in modern, portable C code. To share a variable across different compilation units, a declaration in a designated header in combination with a single definition seems to be a strictly better alternative to me.
While both `int foo;` and `int foo = 42;` are variable definitions, compilers may still make a distinction by way of this (very common) extension (i.e. it technically violates the single definition rule) to standard C (excerpt from `man clang`):

```
-fcommon, -fno-common
              This flag specifies that variables without initializers get common linkage.  It can be  dis-
              abled with -fno-common.
```

Using this compiler switch also solves the linker error:

```
❯ CFLAGS=-fno-common CXXFLAGS=-fno-common CPPFLAGS=-fno-common make -j8 -f examples/Makefile-posix
<snip>
❯ nm build/x86_64-apple-darwin/examples/platforms/posix/libopenthread-posix.a | grep _gPlatformPseudoResetWasRequested
000000000000112c S _gPlatformPseudoResetWasRequested
                 U _gPlatformPseudoResetWasRequested
❯ cd build/x86_64-apple-darwin
build/x86_64-apple-darwin❯ make check
<snip>
  CXX      test_diag.o
  CXXLD    test-diag
  CXX      test_ncp_buffer.o
  CXXLD    test-ncp-buffer
/Applications/Xcode.app/Contents/Developer/usr/bin/make  check-TESTS
PASS: test-aes
<snip>
```

So I think that if global variables _must_ be used, direct initialization will make sure they will not have common linkage, while also making sure that they are always properly initialized. Adding `-fno-common` or `-Wl,-load_all` (or equivalent) moves the fix to the build system and is compatible with the former.

P.S.: While I have found a number of solutions to make the problem go away, I am still not sure _why_ the linker refuses to load the misc.o object file all by itself and am really curious if this is normal behavior. :)

```

- code coverage: how to run analysis; how to view html output?
- doxygen: how to regenerate the docs?
- check: how to run certification tests; how to configure to run against hardware vs. simulator?

issue_title: Support for binary and plain in http device client is half-finished
issue_body:
Reviewing the code:

We support 4 content types in HttpAbstractClient:

```
        # Default content type is json
        contentType = "application/json"
        if dataFormat == "text":
            contentType = "text/plain; charset=utf-8"
        elif dataFormat == "xml":
            contentType = "application/xml"
        elif dataFormat == "bin":
            contentType = "application/octet-stream"
```

But the device.HttpClient only registers encoder modules for xml and json, which means there's no way to actually use `plain` or `bin`:

```
        self.setMessageEncoderModule('json', jsonCodec)
        self.setMessageEncoderModule('xml', xmlCodec)
```

issue_title: Remote execution fails when local_repo is too big
issue_body:
### Observed behavior

When the directory specified by the local_repo dir is too big, remote execution fails with the confusing message "taskflow.exceptions.StorageFailure: Storage backend internal error". This is a frequent error case if the directory pointed to is actually a git repo (i.e., also contains associated git metadata).

### Expected behavior

Artman should validate that the size of local_repo is < 1 MB.

### Action

Accumulate the size of local_repo here: https://github.com/googleapis/artman/blob/e83b08313c7fff2cb1f5ca6f716220050d1d4fd9/execute_pipeline.py#L221
and throw a meaningful exception if it is too big.

issue_title: Upgrade driver for changes from using Tx ID as Asset ID
issue_body:
From https://github.com/bigchaindb/bigchaindb/pull/926/, the interface for `Transaction.transfer()` has changed to take in a `AssetLink` instance rather than an `Asset`.

The ramifications of this should be limited; I think only offchain.py needs to be slightly modified.

**Note**: This should be done on top of #199.

    STATUS:CONFIRMED

And change it to

    STATUS:CANCELLED

In [section 4.8.1.11](https://www.ietf.org/rfc/rfc2445.txt) it says:

```
   Format Definition: The property is defined by the following notation:

     status     = "STATUS" statparam] ":" statvalue CRLF

     statparam  = *(";" xparam)

     statvalue  = "TENTATIVE"           ;Indicates event is
                                        ;tentative.
                / "CONFIRMED"           ;Indicates event is
                                        ;definite.
                / "CANCELLED"           ;Indicates event was
                                        ;cancelled.
        ;Status values for a "VEVENT"
```

However, when one does `vdirsyncer sync` after this it produces the above error.


I'm not 100% sure what the expected behavior here would be. For a one-time event I'm pretty sure that it should be deleted, but I'm not sure about a recurring event. For instance, we have a standing appointment every week, but this week it was cancelled. Only the one occurrence, though.

issue_title: Installation/user guide issues
issue_body:
I followed the instructions over in the [user guide](https://letsencrypt.readthedocs.org/en/latest/using.html#installation) for installation and running the client. The script *immediately* ran apt-get update, which surprised me--I expected a prompt of some type telling me what it was going to do first, possibly offering options.

- There should be some way of bypassing the repo update and software installation without commenting out stuff from the bootstrap file, as some systems (like my WD MyCloud NAS, as an example) break when you run these things; the user should be able to install the dependencies manually before running the automated script. This can be solved in several ways:
  - Provide a parameter to bypass the install entirely
  - Check to see if it's installed, and require an optional parameter to continue with installation, otherwise abort
  - Provide an interactive mode that prompts the user.

- `./letsencrypt-auto --help` doesn't provide any help or other information until *after* client installation. This help should be available *before* the client is installed, possibly with a notice that the software needs to be installed before any commands are available. Given that it runs from the repo, it should be relatively simple to extract the help info from cli.py without actually running it.

- The User Guide has an example command in the [webroot section](https://letsencrypt.readthedocs.org/en/latest/using.html#webroot) that doesn't wrap in small windows and the css is preventing scroll bars. Screenshot [here](http://vgy.me/LULkqY.png).

issue_title: Don't add extra ServerName/ServerAlias directives
issue_body:
With #2537, we correctly detect wildcard `ServerAlias` directives when choosing a vhost, however, we still add a specific `ServerName/ServerAlias` even though it's not necessary.

For example if we have:

```
ServerAlias *.example.com
```

After we install the certifiicate we'll have:

```
ServerAlias *.example.com
ServerAlias www.example.com
```

We shouldn't do this as the change is unnecessary and (slightly) complicates the resulting vhost.

issue_labels: [{'id': 630111673, 'url': 'https://api.github.com/repos/certbot/certbot/labels/area:%20python', 'name': 'area: python', 'color': 'c5def5', 'default': False}, {'id': 200596079, 'url': 'https://api.github.com/repos/certbot/certbot/labels/area:%20ui%20/%20ux', 'name': 'area: ui / ux', 'color': 'c5def5', 'default': False}, {'id': 146954787, 'url': 'https://api.github.com/repos/certbot/certbot/labels/bug', 'name': 'bug', 'color': 'fc2929', 'default': True}, {'id': 146954790, 'url': 'https://api.github.com/repos/certbot/certbot/labels/help%20wanted', 'name': 'help wanted', 'color': '159818', 'default': True}]
issue_body:
https://community.letsencrypt.org/t/attempting-to-generate-cert-getting-unicodeerror-encoding-with-idna-codec-failed/61675

This prevents account registration in this case when Certbot is run with Python 3. It wasn't a problem in Python 2, which doesn't crash under these circumstances.

Do we want to stop using `socket.getfqdn()`, or do we want to ask the Python developers to change this behavior? (The hostnames in question *are* invalid according to the DNS specifications, so it's not quite clear what the proper behavior should be from the point of view of the `socket` library.)

We could also catch the specific exception and provide a more useful error to the user.

issue_title: add structured logging to basescript
Add keyword argument to the check function for max number of messages per error

Right now we have a hard maximum of 5 messages per error, but it would be nice to have this configurable.

Let's make `check` have a keyword argument that, if it's a positive integer, specifies the max number of messages per error, and if it's 0 then all messages appear.

Also would be good to add to the current message something like "Only first X errors shown" if the number of messages exceeds the specified number.

issue_title: Allow third parties to update the counterparty transfer
issue_body:
## Problem Definition

To support offline nodes without closing the netting channels a monitoring third party is required, the third party will be responsible to call `updateTransfer` (or a equivalent function) on behalf of the offline counter party.

## Solution

Allow nodes that are not participants to call a function that has a similar behavior to `updateTransfer`.

## Tasklist

- [ ] A new message format for a node to inform third parties about it's known latest balance proofs.
- [ ] A new netting channel function for a third party to update the transfer on behalf of the participant.
  - [ ] Forbid the third-party from overwriting a participant provided transfer. (Note: This is not to protect against bad behaving participants, but to protect against bad behaving third-parties)
- [ ] unit tests for this.

## Details

The concept of dispute and penalization were removed because:

- only the participants were allowed to update the netting channel by providing the *counter-party* message, this meant messages are always pristine.
- messages are monotonically increasing in value, this gives a strong interest for the participant to provide the latest know message from the counter-party.

With the introduction of third-parties we lose the property of always having pristine messages, but as long there is one well-behaved and up-to-date third party everything is okay. At the same hand we cannot add penalization to third-parties that provide an out-of-date message because the node could intentionally not update the service.

## Related

~Monitor Channels & Dispute #82~
Allow third parties to close the channel #182

issue_title: Save as optimized svg generates invalid svg output
issue_body:
Steps to reproduce bug:

1. Create new document in inkscape
2. Click the text tool
3. Write something, press return, write something, press return, write something
4. Select the three lines of text and set a new font and font-size
5. Save as optimized svg, and make sure the "group collapsing" option is unchecked, and the "create groups for similar attributes" is checked

The generated output svg has a structure like this:

<svg>
  <text>
    <g>
      <tspan>...</tspan>
      <tspan>...</tspan>
      <tspan>...</tspan>
    </g>
  </text>
</svg>

The g tag in this case makes browsers not render the tspans. It's also invalid svg markup, since <g> isn't allowed as a child of <text>.

issue_title: Check if ROI type is correctly set when reading from DICOM
issue_body:
We have currently:

1. https://github.com/pytrip/pytrip/blob/v1.2.0/pytrip/vdx.py#L82

class `VdxCube`,  method `read_dicom`:
```
  v.read_dicom(dcm.RTROIObservations[i], item)
```

2. https://github.com/pytrip/pytrip/blob/v1.2.0/pytrip/vdx.py#L772

class `Voi`,  method `read_dicom`:
```
        self.type = self.get_roi_type_number(np.typename)
```

Why `np.typename` ? `np` if for numpy.

```
>>> help(np.typename)
typename(char)
    Return a description for the given data type code.
```

This seems no to make any sense.

3. https://github.com/pytrip/pytrip/blob/v1.2.0/pytrip/vdx.py#L741

class `Voi`,  method `get_roi_type_number`:

```
    def get_roi_type_number(self, type_name):
        """
        :returns: 1 if GTV or CTV, else 0.
        """
        if type_name == 'EXTERNAL':
            return 0  # TODO: should be 10?
        elif type_name == 'AVOIDANCE':
            return 0
        elif type_name == 'ORGAN':
            return 0
        elif type_name == 'GTV':
            return 1
        elif type_name == 'CTV':
            return 1
        else:
            return 0
```

4. on the other hand, in TRiP98 documentation (http://bio.gsi.de/DOCS/TRiP98/DOCS/dcm2trip.README.TXT) we find:

```
The vdx files must be edited manually with a text editor. Lines beginning
with the keyword "voi" indicate VOIs. dcm2trip assigns "type 1" to all VOIs.
Instead VOI types should be:

1 for the target
2 for the OARs
10 for the external contour
```

issue_title: Management of external jobs.
issue_body:
This comes out of #436 (waiting for the completion of jobs).

Right now each sos step waits for the completion of its tasks. This blocks the current step. I would propose the following way of handing external jobs:

1. A global external job queue is created. It can be a subprocess of sos, or a sos daemon that manages tasks for multiple sos instances. The daemon method is better in that it allows external monitoring of sos jobs.
2. When a step encounters one or more tasks, it simply pushes the tasks to the job queue. The task queue is controlled by `-j` (max jobs).
3. The step would immediately return and mark the step as `busy` in the DAG.
4. The DAG would continue to find runnable steps. 
5. It there is no runnable step, sos could exit with the `--no-wait` option (perhaps leaving a daemon process behind to monitor and submit jobs). This basically means all runnable jobs have been submitted. There might be remaining jobs but they have to wait for the completion of existing jobs. Because `-n` (dryrun) is taken, we might have to use make no-wait the default and use `-w` (wait) for waiting.
6. sos could assume, building another DAG and try to submit jobs. Some jobs would be reported as completed and some jobs would be running. SoS would continue to submit jobs, and perhaps exit again with `--no-wait`.
7. At some point all jobs are done and sos would complete everything.

The `no-wait` mode is important for interactive use because sos would submit the job and return, allowing users to continue with other analysis. The `wait` mode will allow sos to go through the DAG without having to start multiple times.

issue_title: Better interaction with remote running jobs
issue_body:
Say we started a remote job and the local job gets killed. When we re-running the local job, it will connect the remote server and found that it cannot create the job (failed to get a lock because the job is running) and fails.

It would be better if we can recognize the running jobs and wait for it to complete.

issue_body:
With the direct correspondence between `.ipynb` and `.sos` files and the ability to execute `.pynb` files directly (#553),  the structure magics become the only ones that cannot be supported by the `.ipynb` format. We can claim that these are batch mode only features but I do not like the fact that they break `.sos` -> `.ipynb` conversion.

Since I have very rarely used these features, I would propose that we remove them for now, at least from the documentation.

Gao: Let me know if these features are useful to you.

issue_title: User should be able to add strings to a Trie after instantiation
issue_body:
As a user, I want to create an instance of `Trie` and add words to it one-at-a-time, so that I can use a `Trie` in a streaming environment (in which strings arrive on-the-fly). For example,

```python
>>> from marise import Trie
>>> trie = Trie()
>>> trie.add(u'key1')
>>> trie.add(u'key12')
>>> u'key1' in trie
True
>>> u'key12' in trie
True
>>> u'key2' in trie
False
```

This also makes `Trie` behave more like a `set` of strings.

issue_title: Speed up qdac initialisation
issue_body:
Currently setting up a station containing a qdac is very slow.

This is due to getting voltage, voltage_range, current, and current_range for all channels. The time spend seems to be evenly split between these 4 parameters. There are at least to ways of speeding this up. 

* Make the current related parameters optional (and probably off by default). Should give a factor of 2
* Don't ask for voltages for each channel individually. The QDaC can return all voltages in one command which is much faster. This may be fast enough to not need the removal of currents above. 
There is already partial support for this in the channelised version of the QDac driver when asking for more than one channel voltage but it needs to be integrated into the snapshot functions.

issue_title: Fatal error in actor test.
issue_body:
I can't reproduce it reliably, but occasionally I can cause `testInheritActorFromClass` in `actor_test.py` to fail by running the following.

```
for i in {1..500}; do python test/actor_test.py ActorInheritance.testInheritActorFromClass; done
```

I have seen it fail with a check failure in line 504 in `local_scheduler.cc`, which is

```
CHECK(ActorID_equal(TaskSpec_actor_id(spec), NIL_ACTOR_ID));
```

Object reconstruction should be suppressed before it gets to this point. In this particular test, no objects should be evicted and no processes should be failing.

It is a little unclear what is happening here.

issue_title: We should have some kind of better closure tracking for Queues
issue_labels: [{'id': 523592585, 'url': 'https://api.github.com/repos/python-trio/trio/labels/design%20discussion', 'name': 'design discussion', 'color': '0052cc', 'default': False}, {'id': 523592775, 'url': 'https://api.github.com/repos/python-trio/trio/labels/missing%20piece', 'name': 'missing piece', 'color': 'fbca04', 'default': False}, {'id': 538689361, 'url': 'https://api.github.com/repos/python-trio/trio/labels/potential%20API%20breaker', 'name': 'potential API breaker', 'color': 'b60205', 'default': False}]
issue_body:
Right now, if you want to do clean shutdown of a `Queue`, you have to do some messy thing where you pass in a sentinel value and the other side has to watch for it. This is inherited from Python's `queue.Queue`, but ... `queue.Queue` sometimes feels like a neat v1 that never got fully reviewed or polished. (We already got rid of some of its unwise decisions, see #322.)

For streams, there's a nice clear well-defined way to do this: the sender closes the stream, the receiver gets a notification (a special sentinel value in that case), any further attempts to send on the stream raise an error. Or, the receiver closers their end of the stream, and then any further attempts to send/receive raise an error.

For `Queue` it's a little more complicated, because we have the sending and receiving merged into a single object. The most important case is a sender notifying the receiver that they're done. So the simplest thing would be to have a `close` method that makes further calls to `get`/`put` raise an error, and `__anext__` to raise `StopAsyncIteration`, and document that generally it should be the sender that uses it.

(What if there are multiple tasks putting to the same queue? We could have `close` cause any blocked `put`s to immediately wake up (compare #460), or have an `aclose` that waits for any current `put` calls to finish.)

Here's another option, that's more disruptive but might ultimately be better: have the `put` and `get` sides be two different objects, so you'd do something like `put_queue, get_queue = trio.open_queue()`. `put_queue` would have the `put` methods, `get_queue` would have the `get` methods and async iteration protocol, and they could be closed independently, with the same detailed semantics as the stream APIs.

Maybe we'd even want to make an ABC for this, like `ObjectSendStream` and `ObjectReceiveStream` (names to be bikeshedded later); then people could make versions that use pickle to implement the interface on top of a `Stream`, etc. If doing this we might want to also change the names to better match the `Stream` conventions, like have the two main methods be `send` and `receive` instead of `put` and `get`.

But we shouldn't take this analogy *too* far... regular in-memory `Queue`s are often used in a way that byte streams aren't. Are there any cases where what you really want is a *bidirectional* `Queue`? If so then that would be a strong argument for splitting the two endpoints into two different objects... but I'm not sure if this really happens? Another way `Queue`s are different from streams is that fan-in and fan-out is common. For fan-in/fan-out, maybe we could do even better than having two objects: maybe we want to have lots of objects, one for every send/receiver! For example this might look like:

```python
async def sender(object_send_stream):
    async with object_send_stream:
        await object_send_stream.send(None)

async def receiver(object_receive_stream):
    async with object_receive_stream:
        async for obj in object_receive_stream:
            print("got", None)

async def main():
    object_stream_buffer = trio.ObjectStreamBuffer()
    async with trio.open_nursery() as nursery:
        nursery.start_soon(sender, object_stream_buffer.object_send_stream())
        nursery.start_soon(sender, object_stream_buffer.object_send_stream())
        nursery.start_soon(sender, object_stream_buffer.object_send_stream())
        nursery.start_soon(receiver, object_stream_buffer.object_receive_stream())
```

(Expected output: prints `got None` three times, and then exits. Notice that the three calls to `sender` each get their own `object_send_stream`, and each of them closes their version when done.)

The idea here is that by having multiple send/receive endpoints that you can attach to the same underlying buffer, you can track the closure state for each endpoint separately. So for a fan-in case, you can have a bunch of producers that each have their own copy of the send endpoint, send stuff for a while, then close their endpoint, and then *once all the endpoints are closed* the receiver gets notified. (This could even replace the `task_left` bookkeeping in [this example](https://gitter.im/python-trio/general?at=5ad186345d7286b43a29af53).)

I guess another way to do this would be to have the `put_queue, get_queue = trio.open_queue()` style API, but have a `.clone()` method on the endpoints. This would enforce that you can't create a new endpoint unless there's already at least one open endpoint, which might help avoid race conditions like: `send_stream1 = buffer.object_send_stream(); send_stream1.close(); send_stream2 = buffer.object_send_stream(); send_stream2.send(...)` (fails because after the first call to `close`, there were zero endpoints, so the underlying buffer got closed). Or else I guess we could "closed" be a toggleable state – it starts off closed, until you create the first endpoint? But then that has a different race condition where if the receiver starts first they might see the stream as being closed and exit before the first producer wakes up and starts sending stuff.

Hmm.

CC: @oremanj since they reminded me of this today.

issue_title: use a heap instead of sorteddict?

I see trio depends on sortedcontainers solely because it needs a priority queue for sorting deadlines, and the Python standard library has one for that exact purpose.

issue_title: OpenCensusServerInterceptor reports incorrect timing
issue_body:
There is a bug in OpenCensusServerInterceptor that causes server spans to be finished immediately. You can reproduce it by running the helloworld grpc example in examples/trace/grpc. You should see a server span with a very short execution time, for example when I run it locally with a print exporter I get:

`{'traceId': 'a8fdeb7813144b178cb4b3d3d9b8fee4', 'spans': [{'childSpanCount': 0, 'displayName': {'truncated_byte_count': 0, 'value': 'grpc_server'}, 'parentSpanId': 4417962580138865, 'startTime': '2018-01-23T16:01:17.826761Z', 'attributes': {'attributeMap': {'/component': {'string_value': {'truncated_byte_count': 0, 'value': 'grpc'}}}}, 'spanId': 7098108131070600, 'endTime': '2018-01-23T16:01:17.826771Z'}]}`

The problem is that [this line](https://github.com/census-instrumentation/opencensus-python/blob/master/opencensus/trace/ext/grpc/server_interceptor.py#L62) is being traced, but it isn't actually executing the server handler code. It just returns an RPCMethodHandler, which defers execution. 

I hacked up a [quick fix](https://gist.github.com/wkiser/d086a3f623f135265914e2b51bb722e8) that wraps the RPCMethodHandler and instruments the span when the handler is evaluated. A better solution might be to have the RpcMethodHandlerWrapper accept arbitrary callbacks, or to update grpc to have RPCMethodHandler accept callbacks natively.

Happy to submit a PR if you have a preferred approach. Thanks!

issue_title: file_exporter: how to dump data every x minutes
issue_body:
Hi,

I would be interested in using the `file_exporter` to dump the instrumentation metrics that the `opencensus` library provides, every 1 min or so.

That is useful in case you have multiple instances of the same process on the same or different host (for example, you can differentiate between them using the hostname or the pid).
If I were to use the prometheus exporter that would need a separate port number for each process, which is something obviously you cannot work with.

This request is inspired by the `pycounters` library here https://bitbucket.org/bleskes/pycounters/src/2db4d3b706c82e9f44b29dd24cca6eeead00d941/src/pycounters/base.py?at=default&fileviewer=file-view-default

It creates a thread that according to the defined schedule it dumps the instrumentation metrics.
Which is a very nice decoupled (from the backend) mechanism to report the instrumentation metrics, 
without forcing you to explicitly call the reporting of the metrics, which is not always possible when you loop forever and very very often.

Would you be so kind to advise if this is supported or what is the equivalent way of achieving the same using opencensus ?

issue_title: RAM issues with the database solution
issue_body:
I've been following several threads (including #529 )  with @tendres and exchanging ideas. We have been slowly increasing the size of our data set testing scalability of dedupe and postgres.

While early results looked promising we have run into a limitation with the database solution.
It appears that the maximum number of records dedupe can process is directly correlated to the amount of RAM that is available for the blocking phase. We are seeing for every 10000 records blocked an increase sometimes over 100MB - our last attempt we saw 50GB of RAM slowly consumed before the system hung and the process needed to be killed. We actually recorded the process when it hung [Dedupe Video](https://www.dropbox.com/s/nykvr0wjpjxzedt/dedupe.mov?dl=0).

Appears to occur consistently when reaching mergeScores and/or scoreDuplicates which produces the score_chunk process...

Any thoughts on what we're running into here? Is this expected behavior and thus the solution would be adding more RAM?

[dedupe.mov.zip](https://github.com/dedupeio/dedupe/files/1019575/dedupe.mov.zip)

issue_title: Make time as a required component for streaming DTC validator
Negative indexing in slice
issue_body:
Currently, we are doing Caffe2 behavior, which says negative indexes are inclusive. This allows you to pass 0:-1 to indicate that the entire range should be taken.

HOWEVER, this is inconsistent with Numpy behavior.

```
>>> import numpy
>>> x = numpy.ndarray(shape=(2,4))
>>> x[0:-1, 1:3]
array([[  5.21625023e-317,   1.16138128e-012]])
>>> x[0:, 1:3]                                                                                                  
array([[  5.21625023e-317,   1.16138128e-012],
       [  2.16449511e+233,   1.95218670e-110]])
```

This is because Python's slice syntax permits omitting the ending index to say 'go to the end'.  But in the format we've picked (IntTensor for starts and ends), this is not representable.

My suggestion for now is that we disallow negative indexing for now, pending a resolution of this problem.

issue_title: Feature request:TL implement of CornerPooling
issue_body:
`class CornetPool(Layer):
    """The :class:`CornetPool` class is 2D cornet pool, see `here <https://arxiv.org/abs/1808.01244/>`__.

    Parameters
    --------------
    prev_layer : :class:`Layer`
        Previous layer.
    filter_size : int
        The filter size.
    mode:str
        BottomRight for the top left corrnet,
        TopLeft for the bottom right corrnet.
    name : str
        A unique layer name.
    """

    def __init__(
        self,
        prev_layer = None,
        filter_size=(3,3),
        mode='BottomRight',
        name ='cornerpool_layer',
    ):
        Layer.__init__(self, prev_layer=prev_layer, name=name)
        self.inputs = prev_layer.outputs
        if mode=='BottomRight':
            temp=tf.keras.layers.ZeroPadding2D(padding=((0, filter_size[0]-1), (0,  filter_size[1]-1)), name=name)(self.inputs)
            temp=tf.layers.max_pooling2d(temp, (filter_size[0],1), (1,1), padding='valid', data_format='channels_last')
            self.outputs=tf.layers.max_pooling2d(temp, (1,filter_size[1]), (1,1), padding='valid', data_format='channels_last',name=name)
        elif mode=='TopLeft':
            temp=tf.keras.layers.ZeroPadding2D(padding=((filter_size[0]-1,0), (filter_size[1]-1,0)), name=name)(self.inputs)
            temp=tf.layers.max_pooling2d(temp, (filter_size[0],1), (1,1), padding='valid', data_format='channels_last')
            self.outputs=tf.layers.max_pooling2d(temp, (1,filter_size[1]), (1,1), padding='valid', data_format='channels_last',name=name)
        else:
            raise AssertionError("Mode should be of 'BottomRight'and'TopLeft' ")
        self._add_layers(self.outputs)`

Maybe Zero Padding is not a rigorous method for leaky relu activation func?

issue_title: add Graph.fromN3
issue_body:
drewpca, 2008-12-26T22:31:59.000Z 
It would help the creation of our test cases, rdflib's test cases, bug
reports, and tutorials if there was a one-liner to make a graph initialized
from some N3. I propose this API:

from rdflib.Graph import Graph
graph = Graph.fromN3("""
@prefix ...

""")

Comment 1 by lindstr...@gmail.com
In trunk, I think the 'data' parameter to 'Graph.parse' is good enough. Use like::

    from rdflib.graph import Graph
    graph = Graph( format="n3", data="""@prefix ... """)

Can this issue thus be closed? (Sure, the relevant tests should be adapted to use this as well..)


Comment 2 by drewpca
Yes, that's equivalent to what I was looking for. Thanks for the update.

Should we have a label for 'put this in the release notes'?

In rdflib/syntax/parsers/ntriples.py

Line 149, in readline() method:
      while True:
         m = r_line.match(self.buffer)
         if m: # the more likely prospect
            self.buffer = self.buffer[m.end():]
            return m.group(1)
         else:
            buffer = self.file.read(bufsiz)
            if not buffer and not self.buffer.isspace():
                raise ParseError("EOF in line")
            elif not buffer:

            self.buffer += buffer
